feedback.text: I urge you to consider treating a video games as any other products regarding longevity. If you buy a car, the manufacturer cannot disable it just because they stop making parts or updates, and in the same way there should be this consideration regarding video games. This is especially regarding games which is single player or don't really have to rely on online connection, yet some require it. Some have online play, which could be made so we can setup a server ourselves or over direct connection with others. Some types like MMO and Live Service games is more difficult, but as we have spent money on these games and we have a game. There should still be a way to play the games in some offline form or other online form in case the support stops from the developers. Fair digital regulation should allow fallback options when official support end. Just as we do in other industries with discontinued products. Furthermore video games are a form of art and culture, not just consumable entertainment. When companies shut down servers and remove access, they erase part of digital history. Without legal protections, entire genres and gaming communities can vanish, leaving future generations unable to experience or study them. To those who believe video games are not art, then look into how much work there goes into motion capture, world designs, music, dialogue, story. We got book authors, musicians, song writers, actors and more coming into video games each year. Which only supports the idea that this is a combined form of many art forms. A fair digital marketplace must ensure that when you buy a game, you own a usable product, not a unknown timed licensed which can be disabled any time by the publishers or developers.
